notes.anchor: money was decreed to Pompey for the corn-supply up to forty million sesterces
notes.note: Pompey held an extraordinary five-year command over Rome's grain supply (cura annonae), granted in 57 BC; here the Senate votes him 40 million sesterces to fund it amid a shortage and high prices.

notes.anchor: the Capitolines and the Mercuriales threw M. Furius Flaccus
notes.note: The Capitolini and Mercuriales were two Roman religious colleges (collegia), tied to the cults of Capitoline Jupiter and of Mercury. Their joint expulsion of the knight M. Furius Flaccus was a public disgrace.

notes.anchor: I gave the betrothal-feast to Crassipes
notes.note: The sponsalia was the Roman betrothal ceremony, sealing the engagement with a feast. Furius Crassipes was about to marry Cicero's daughter Tullia.
Pompey's horti, his suburban pleasure-gardens, where Cicero called on him before leaving Rome. Such gardens were grand estates on the city's edge, used for receiving clients and conducting business.

notes.anchor: to lodge that day at T. Titius's house in the Anagnine country
notes.note: Cicero breaks his journey south at the estate of T. Titius near Anagnia, a Hernican town in Latium, on the way to his and Quintus's family properties around Arpinum.

notes.anchor: and to be the day after at Laterium
notes.note: Laterium and Arcanum (below) were estates of Quintus Cicero near Arpinum, both then being built or improved; Cicero was inspecting the family's properties on his brother's behalf.
